coredata.dc:title: Facile gram-scale synthesis of hydrophobic carbon quantum dots (CQDs) as a sustainable reinforcement for basalt fibre-reinforced polymer composites

coredata.dc:description: Nanocomposites offer superior mechanical properties, making sustainable nano-carbon reinforcements increasingly attractive due to their affordability and high specific strength. Carbon quantum dots (CQDs) are particularly promising due to their abundant precursors and tunable interfacial properties. However, traditional CQD synthesis often involves complex purification methods with low production yields and results in highly polar CQDs, limiting their compatibility with non-polar polymer matrices. To address these limitations, this work introduces hydrophobic CQDs with a production yield exceeding 85 % through a facile filtration-based purification process. These hydrophobic CQDs were readily dispersed in unsaturated polyester resin using simple mechanical stirring and sonication. The optimal inclusion of 0.5 wt% CQDs led to significant enhancements in tensile strength (72 %), flexural strength (43 %), and impact strength (51 %) compared to the pristine composite. Thermal characterization revealed that these improvements can be attributed to the effective modulation of the polymer microstructure, even at low CQD concentrations. Superior specific strength and enhanced toughness of CQDs reinforced composite are attractive attributes for their application in automotive body parts, impact resistant sports gear, and ballistic protection.

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was supported by the National Research Foundation of Korea (NRF) grant funded by the Korea government (MSIT) (No. NRF-2021R1A2C2094794 and RS-2024-00408729 ). This work was supported by the ERC Center funded by the National Research Foundation of Korea RS-2022-NR070840 ).

MumtazAli boasts a rich academic and research background. Currently, he is serving as a Research Professor at Hanyang University, funded by the Brain Pool Program Korea. Before this, he served as an Assistant Professor at National Textile University, Faislabad Pakistan with an experience of 3 years. His contributions in research earned him several national research grants, focused on the application of quantum dots in textiles and polymers. In recognition of his outstanding PhD thesis, he was honoured with the International Carbon Award in 2022 for \u201CAdvancement in Carbon Science and Technology\u201D. This award also granted him an extended advisory position with Elsevier Carbon journal (IF 11.5). Based on his distinguished research, he received the \u201CBest Researcher Award\u201D from Hanyang University in 2020. Currently, focus of his current research lies in scalable and green synthesis of low dimensional carbon-rich catalysts, to replace metallic catalysts.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Illustration of the synthesis process for CQDs, depicting the structural evolution at each stage.
Schematic illustration of basalt fiber composite fabrication using CQDs modified unsaturated polyester resin.
Characterizations of CQDs using (a) TEM, (b) XRD, (c) XPS, and (d) FTIR.
Mechanical characterizations of CQDs reinforced composites: (a) tensile strength curve, (b) tensile modulus, (c) flexural strength curve, and (d) flexural modulus.
Comparison of thermal characteristics of composites, via (a) DMA (tan δ), (b) DSC, and (c) CTE.

SEM analysis of (a) neat composite and (b) 0.5 wt% CQDs-reinforced composite. Inset scale bars: 300 µm (low magnification) and 50 µm (high magnification). (c) XRD analysis of composite showing higher crystallinity by incorporation of 0.5 % CQDs. (d & e) Mechanism of enhanced mechanical properties by incorporation of CQDs, depicted based on SEM results. (d) Larger micro-domains of neat sample allow easier crack propagation and (e) smaller microdomains and compact structure of CQDs modified resin is responsible for suppressing crack propagation.
Mechanical properties of composites with different concentrations of CQDs, including tensile modulus, flexural modulus, and impact strength.

head.ce:title: Facile gram-scale synthesis of hydrophobic carbon quantum dots (CQDs) as a sustainable reinforcement for basalt fibre-reinforced polymer composites

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Nanocomposites offer superior mechanical properties, making sustainable nano-carbon reinforcements increasingly attractive due to their affordability and high specific strength. Carbon quantum dots (CQDs) are particularly promising due to their abundant precursors and tunable interfacial properties. However, traditional CQD synthesis often involves complex purification methods with low production yields and results in highly polar CQDs, limiting their compatibility with non-polar polymer matrices. To address these limitations, this work introduces hydrophobic CQDs with a production yield exceeding 85 % through a facile filtration-based purification process. These hydrophobic CQDs were readily dispersed in unsaturated polyester resin using simple mechanical stirring and sonication. The optimal inclusion of 0.5 wt% CQDs led to significant enhancements in tensile strength (72 %), flexural strength (43 %), and impact strength (51 %) compared to the pristine composite. Thermal characterization revealed that these improvements can be attributed to the effective modulation of the polymer microstructure, even at low CQD concentrations. Superior specific strength and enhanced toughness of CQDs reinforced composite are attractive attributes for their application in automotive body parts, impact resistant sports gear, and ballistic protection.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Fiber-reinforced polymer composites (FRPCs) have gained significant demand in various industries, including automotive, aerospace, and sports, due to their high specific strength and mechanical properties. However, the relatively low out-of-plane properties of FRPCs, stemming from weak interlaminar interfaces, remain a significant limitation [1]. To address this issue, various techniques, such as 3D weaving [2], stitching, z-pinning [3], and the incorporation of nanofillers [4], have been explored. Nanofillers enhance the mechanical properties of FRPCs and modify their thermal and functional properties [1,4,5,6,7,8,9]. By improving the mechanical strength of resin and interfacial adhesion between fibers and the matrix, nanofillers can significantly enhance the out-of-plane properties of FRPCs. Additionally, the incorporation of nanofillers can modulate the microstructure of the polymer matrix, leading to increased toughness and resistance to crack propagation.

The matrix is the weaker component of a composite, enhancing its mechanical properties is crucial for improving the overall performance of FRPCs. Nano-reinforcements, particularly carbon-based nanomaterials, offer a promising solution due to their high strength, stiffness, and lightweight nature [10]. The hierarchical structure formed by the combination of nano- and micro-reinforcements (fibers) synergistically improves the plasticity and toughness of FRPCs [11]. This hierarchical structure enables efficient stress transfer and energy dissipation, leading to improved impact resistance and damage tolerance [11]. Carbon-based nanomaterials, such as carbon nanotubes and carbon black, provide a sustainable and affordable alternative to traditional reinforcing materials, such as TiO2, SiO2, and ZnO [12]. Their high surface area, tunable properties, and strong interfacial adhesion with polymer matrices make them ideal candidates for a wide range of applications. For instance, CNTs have been employed to enhance the mechanical properties of aerospace components, while carbon black is widely used as a filler in tires to improve their wear resistance and traction.

By carefully selecting the type and concentration of carbon nanomaterial, it is possible to tailor the properties of polymer composites to meet specific application requirements. Carbon-based nanomaterials have garnered significant attention as potential reinforcements for polymer matrices due to their exceptional mechanical, thermal, and electrical properties [10,13]. The choice of nanomaterial is crucial, as it directly influences the resulting properties of the composite. For instance, graphene, with its high aspect ratio and excellent conductivity, is well-suited for enhancing the thermal and electrical conductivity of polymer composites [14,15]. However, its dispersion in polymer matrices can be challenging, often requiring complex surface modification techniques [16]. In contrast, oxidized graphene oxide, with its hydrophilic nature, is more compatible with hydrophilic polymers [17,18]. While it can improve the mechanical properties of composites, its impact on electrical conductivity is limited. Carbon nanotubes, renowned for their extraordinary strength and stiffness, can significantly enhance the functional and mechanical properties of composites [19,20]. However, their tendency to agglomerate and form bundles can hinder their effective dispersion and limit their potential benefits. The substantial potential of graphene and carbon nanotubes as high-performance nanofillers has been well-established. However, their scalability and cost limitations have shifted research focus towards more sustainable, abundant, and affordable carbon-based materials, such as carbon black and activated charcoal [21]. While these nanofillers can enhance the mechanical properties of composites, including strength and stiffness, they can also induce brittleness. Furthermore, achieving complete dispersion of these nanoparticles often requires complex surface modification techniques, which can be challenging and costly. Moreover, the filtration issues associated with poorly dispersed nanomaterials can hinder their application in vacuum infusion processes.

To overcome the limitations associated with traditional carbon nanomaterials, carbon quantum dots (CQDs) have emerged as a promising alternative [22]. These low-dimensional carbon nanomaterials, with a core size typically less than 10 nm, exhibit a unique combination of sp2 and sp3 hybridized carbon atoms, resulting in exceptional properties. CQDs possess a high surface-to-volume ratio, enabling an intimate interface with the polymer matrix and enhancing interfacial adhesion [22]. Furthermore, their small size allows for uniform dispersion within the polymer matrix, minimizing agglomeration and maximizing reinforcement effects, without filtration during vacuum infiltration. The ability of CQDs to significantly enhance the mechanical properties of polymer composites, even at extremely low concentrations, is particularly noteworthy [23]. CQDs can limit the growth of large crystalline domains in polymers, thereby reducing stiffness, enhancing flexibility, and doubling the impact strength [23,24]. Additionally, CQDs can influence the preferential growth of specific crystal phases, further tailoring the mechanical properties of the polymer [25]. CQDs-modified Cu2O nanoparticles incorporated into epoxy resin led to hyperbranched network, resulting in a three-fold increase in elongation at a low loading concentration of 0.5 % [26].

The mechanism underlying enhanced mechanical properties often involves a combination of factors. The CQDs' core serve as crack deflectors, dissipating energy and increasing toughness [26,27]. Simultaneously, surface functional groups promote strong interfacial interactions with the polymer matrix, leading to enhanced crosslinking and improved mechanical properties [23,26,28]. For example, CQDs-modified fly ash has been shown to increase the flexural strength of epoxy composites by 71 % at a loading of 0.1 %, attributed to the formation of a compact polymer structure [29,30]. Two fold enhanced toughness of polyvinyl alcohol [31] and 50 % increased elongation of chitosan film was achieved with citric acid-derived CQDs inclusions [32]. Previous studies have primarily employed hydrophilic CQDs derived from hydrophilic precursors, which are rich in polar functional groups like –OH, –NH, and –C=O [31,32]. While these CQDs are compatible with hydrophilic polymers, their dispersion and interfacial strength with hydrophobic thermoset polymers remain a problem. To address these limitations, CQDs are often integrated with nano-supports to enhance interfacial interactions and improve dispersion [23,26]. However, traditional CQD synthesis methods often require complex purification techniques, such as dialysis and chromatography, which are time-consuming and limit production yield.

To overcome these challenges, we propose a scalable and facile synthesis method for hydrophobic CQDs. By using water as an anti-solvent for hydrophobic CQDs, their aggregates can be readily purified through a simple filtration process. These hydrophobic CQDs exhibit excellent dispersibility in unsaturated polyester resin, eliminating the need for complex dispersion techniques. With an optimal loading of 0.5 %, CQDs significantly enhanced the tensile strength by 72 %, flexural strength by 51 %, and impact strength by 43 % compared to the pristine composite. Notably, these improvements were achieved without compromising elongation, making the proposed composite a promising candidate for high-energy absorption applications.

Thiobenzoic acid, melamine, and acetic acid, sourced from Sigma-Aldrich, were used as precursors for the synthesis of CQDs. Chopped basalt fibers (diameter 6–21 μm, specific gravity 1.75–1.95 g/cm3) were obtained from Kamenny Vek Corporation (Moscow, Russia) and used for non-woven reinforcement fabrication. Commercial-grade unsaturated polyester resin, cobalt octate initiator, and methyl ethyl ketone peroxide accelerator were procured locally.

One-pot hydrothermal synthesis was employed to synthesize CQDs, as shown schematically in Fig. 1 , with a slight variation in the previously reported method [33]. A mixture of 692 mg thiobenzoic acid and 251 mg melamine was dispersed in 50 mL acetic acid using magnetic stirring and water bath sonication for 30 min each. The solution was transferred to a 100 mL Teflon-lined autoclave and heated at 180 °C for 10 h. After cooling to room temperature, the hydrothermal solution was dispersed in 1 L of boiling water and left undisturbed for 30 min to induce CQD aggregation. Due to their hydrophobic nature, the CQD aggregates were easily filtered using a 0.5 μm nylon filter. The filtered CQD cake was dried at 70 °C for 3 h. This process yielded hydrophobic CQDs with high compatibility with most thermoset resins and a production yield exceeding 85 %, enabling the scalable synthesis of nano-reinforcements.

A schematic representation of the composite fabrication process is shown in Fig. 2 . CQDs were initially dispersed in unsaturated polyester resin through 30 min of mechanical stirring at 700 rpm. Subsequently, 30 min of water bath sonication was employed to break down any remaining agglomerates and ensure thorough dispersion of CQDs. Initiator (2 wt%) and accelerator (2 wt%) were mechanically mixed into the CQD-modified resin. The resulting mixture was then impregnated into a basalt fiber non-woven mat (840 GSM) and low compression molding technique was used to achieve uniform resin infusion within the fabric structure. After complete infusion, the composite was pre-cured at room temperature overnight, followed by post-curing at 120 °C for 90 min. Samples were coded according to wt% of CQDs dispersed in the unsaturated polyester resin: neat (0 %, reference sample), 0.125 %, 0.25 %, 0.5 %, and 0.75 wt%.

To confirm the structure and size distribution of CQDs, transmission electron microscopy (TEM): manufacture and model = Hitachi Japan, SU8010 was used. X-ray diffraction (XRD) using a Rigaku D/MAX-2500 X-ray diffractometer with Cu Kα radiation was performed to determine the crystal structure. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) using a multi-scale ESCA 2000 system with an energy step size of 0.05 eV was used for chemical composition analysis. Functional groups of CQDs were analyzed using Fourier transform infra-red (FTIR) spectrophotometer (Thermo Fisher Scientific Nicolett™ iS™ USA) in attenuated total reflection (ATR) mode. CQDs powder was scanned in the 4000–500 cm−1 wavenumber range on the diamond baseline.

To assess the reinforcing effect of CQD inclusions, the mechanical properties of the composites were characterized. Tensile strength was measured according to ASTM D3039 using a Zwick Roell Z100 testing machine with a 100 KN load cell, a gauge length of 120 mm, and a crosshead speed of 2 mm/min. Flexural strength was determined using a 3-point bending test following ASTM D7264 on a Zwick Roell Z100 testing machine with a 20 KN load cell and a crosshead speed of 1 mm/min. Impact strength was evaluated using a Charpy instrumented hammer (25 J) according to ISO 179 on a Zwick/Roell HIT50P impact tester. Dynamic mechanical analysis (DMA) was performed on a PerkinElmer DMA 8000 in 3-point bending mode at a frequency of 5 Hz, a temperature range of 25–120 °C, and a heating rate of 3 °C/min.

To assess the influence of CQD reinforcement on the thermal properties of the composites, several thermal analyses were conducted. The coefficient of thermal expansion (CTE) was measured using a DIL 801 L TA dilatometer to determine dimensional changes in the temperature range of 25–150 °C at a heating rate of 5 °C/min. Differential scanning calorimetry (DSC) was conducted on a PerkinElmer DSC 4000 to analyze heat flow in the temperature range of 25–375 °C at a heating rate of 5 °C/min. Thermogravimetric analysis (TGA) was performed to evaluate thermal stability and degradation kinetics using a PerkinElmer STA 8000. The analysis was conducted over a temperature range of 25–450 °C at a heating rate of 5 °C/min under a nitrogen atmosphere. Field emission scanning electron microscopy (FE-SEM) was performed with NOVA Nano SEM450 microscope, operated at 10 kV. Samples were analyzed from the point of breakage and samples were silver sputtered for 1 min, to obtain clearer image.

Recent advancements in materials science have focused on developing high-performance composites using nano-reinforcements that enhance mechanical properties at low filler content. While traditional fillers like SiO2, TiO2, and ZnO have been widely studied, carbon-based nanomaterials offer superior specific strength and interfacial interactions. However, their tendency to agglomerate remains a major challenge, often compromising composite integrity. To overcome this, we explore hydrophobic carbon quantum dots (CQDs), synthesized via a scalable method, as self-dispersing nano-reinforcements. Their ultra-small size (<10 nm) ensures high surface area and effective integration with polymer matrices, eliminating the need for complex dispersion techniques. TEM images [Fig. 3 (a)] revealed that the CQDs are uniformly dispersed with a near-spherical morphology. The average particle diameter was found to be 5.4 ± 0.3 nm, as determined from the size distribution histogram presented in Fig. S1, based on measurements of over 100 individual quantum dots.

The crystal structure of CQDs was investigated using X-ray diffraction (XRD) [Fig. 3(b)]. Two distinct peaks centered around 25° and 45° were observed, corresponding to the (002) and (100) planes of graphite, respectively. The interplanar spacing of 0.34 nm and 0.21 nm, calculated from these peaks, aligns with previously reported values. The broad and low-intensity peaks are indicative of the small size and high defect density of the synthesized CQDs [34]. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) was employed to analyze the elemental composition of CQDs [Fig. 3(c)]. The survey spectrum revealed prominent peaks attributed to S 2p, S 2 s, C 1 s, N 1 s, and O 1 s, centered at binding energies of 164, 226.5, 284.5, 399.4, and 532 eV, respectively. A quantitative analysis of the elemental composition, as shown in the inset of Fig. 3(c), revealed a dominance of carbon (66 %) and sulfur (16 %). The high-resolution C 1 s XPS spectrum of the CQDs (Fig. S2) reveals that the carbon framework is predominantly composed of C–C, C=C, and C–H bonds, accounting for approximately 82 % of the total carbon content [35]. The remaining 18 % corresponds to heteroatom-doped carbon species, primarily C–N and C–S bonds (Fig. S2), indicative of nitrogen and sulfur incorporation into the CQD structure [36,37]. Compared to hydrophilic CQDs synthesized from precursors such as citric acid [3831], the present hydrophobic CQDs exhibit significantly lower levels of oxygen-containing functionalities. The resulting surface is largely non-polar, which contributes to rapid aggregation in aqueous media due to poor dispersibility.

Fourier-transform infrared (FTIR) spectroscopy was utilized to characterize the functional groups present in the CQDs [Fig. 3(d)]. The spectrum exhibited a high abundance of non-polar functional groups, further confirming the hydrophobic nature of the synthesized CQDs. Key peaks were observed at the following wavenumbers: 2978 and 2823 cm−1 (C–H stretching), 2819 cm−1 (S–H stretching), 2060 cm−1 (C≡N stretching), 1669 cm−1 (O=C–N–H bending), 1465 cm−1 (C=C stretching), 1412 cm−1 (C–N stretching), 1258 cm−1 (C–NH stretching), 1152 cm−1 (C–O stretching), and 548 cm−1 (S–S stretching). The presence of non-polar groups such as –CH3, S–S, and C=C, which are absent in conventional CQDs, contributes significantly to the hydrophobic properties of the synthesized CQDs. Additionally, the absence of hydrophilic groups like O–H and N–H, commonly found in conventional CQDs, further reinforces the hydrophobic nature of the synthesized materials.

Due to the high surface area of CQDs, a significant enhancement of mechanical properties is previously observed at low concentrations. CQDs used for the reinforcement of polymers previously are hydrophilic in nature, therefore their interfacial interactions and dispersion are not up to the mark. In addition, the synthesis of hydrophilic CQDs requires dialysis membrane purification or chromatography, both of which are costly and time-consuming processes. Such complex purification methods limit the scalability and commercial scale applications of CQDs reinforced composites. In contrast, hydrophobic CQDs can resolve the above problems, thus extending CQDs reinforcement applications. Hydrophobic CQDs readily aggregate in water, which can easily be purified using filtration, thus simpler purification method makes the CQDs reinforcements closer to practical applications. Moreover, hydrophobicity promotes compatibility with common thermosetting polymer resins, such as unsaturated polyester and epoxy.

The UV–visible absorption, photoluminescence excitation, and emission spectra of the CQDs film deposited on a glass slide were studied to understand their photophysical properties (Fig. S3). The UV–Vis absorption spectrum displayed prominent peaks from 300–450 nm, corresponding to π–π* and n–π* electronic transitions [Fig. S3(a)] [39,40,41,42]. These high-energy transitions are attributed to the excitation of electrons in the CQD core. A lower energy absorption feature around 550 nm was also observed, associated with molecular states formed by carbon fragments linked via S–S bonds [43]. The N,S-codoped structure introduces excess electrons, facilitating these low-energy transitions. The excitation spectrum showed maximum intensity around 550 nm [Fig. S3(b)], aligned with the aforementioned molecular states. A broad emission peak centered at 589 nm was observed under peak excitation, indicating the presence of size-dependent quantum confinement effects in the synthesized CQDs [43,44,45].

The photoluminescence quantum yield (PLQY) of the CQDs was determined following a previously reported protocol [46]. The CQDs exhibited a solid-state PLQY of 5.2 %, which is notably high given the common quenching effects observed in aggregated or powder forms of CQDs [47]. This emission retention is attributed to the restricted intramolecular rotations around the S–S bonds, which limit non-radiative decay pathways and promote aggregation-induced red-shifted emission, rather than the typical fluorescence quenching observed in similar systems [43].

The hydrophobic nature of the CQDs enables facile dispersion in organic solvents such as acetone, which is also commonly used as a solvent in unsaturated polyester resins. In acetone, the CQDs exhibited blue photoluminescence, as evident from the PL spectrum (Fig. S4[a]). In contrast, when dispersed in water, the CQDs rapidly aggregate due to poor solubility. This aggregation restricts the intramolecular rotations around the disulfide (S–S) bonds, leading to a red-shifted emission (Fig. S4[b]), as reported previously [33]. Interestingly, when the CQDs were dispersed in a water–acetone mixture, both blue and red emission features were observed in the PL spectrum (Fig. S4[c]), suggesting the coexistence of dispersed and aggregated species under these intermediate conditions [33]. The difference in PL emission of the CQDs colloid is obvious under 365 nm UV lamp, as shown in Fig. S4(d).

Enhancement of composite properties through nano-reinforcement is a well-established approach. However, achieving uniform dispersion of nanofillers within the resin matrix often requires complex techniques. This study demonstrates a simple and effective method to disperse self-stabilized CQDs in unsaturated polyester resin. Given the small size of CQDs, their influence on the polymer structure is significant even at low concentrations (0.125–0.75 %). The facile dispersion of CQDs in UP resin is attributed to the presence of similar functional groups, such as –CH3, C=O, C=C, which facilitate Van der Waals interactions. Moreover, the functional groups of CQDs can participate in the chemical crosslinking of the resin matrix. The combined effect of the CQDs' unique structure and morphology results in enhanced mechanical properties of the composites.

To assess the mechanical property enhancements, tensile strength was evaluated using a universal tensile testing machine. Fig. 4 (a) illustrates the elongation-stress curves of the composites, revealing a significant increase in tensile strength upon CQD inclusion. Notably, the maximum tensile strength was achieved at a CQD concentration of 0.5 %, while maintaining a desirable elongation. The tensile strength exhibited a gradual increase with increasing CQD concentration up to 0.5 %, signifying a saturation point. Beyond this concentration, a decrease in tensile strength was observed. The optimal CQD concentration of 0.5 % resulted in a significant 72 % increase in ultimate breaking stress, i.e. 19 MPa. Furthermore, the tensile modulus of the composite was also significantly enhanced by CQD incorporation, as depicted in Fig. 4(b). The reference sample without CQDs inclusion, exhibited a tensile modulus of 312 MPa. The inclusion of CQDs led to a linear increase in modulus up to a concentration of 0.5 %, culminating in a maximum tensile modulus of 591 MPa. This optimal CQD loading yielded a substantial 89 % increase in tensile modulus, underscoring their significant influence on enhancing tensile properties.

When subjected to tensile loading, the weaker matrix phase of the composite typically yields first, followed by load transfer to the reinforcing fibers. Consequently, enhancing the matrix properties is crucial for improving the overall tensile strength of the composite. The incorporation of 0.5 % CQDs successfully achieved this objective. This performance enhancement is attributed to the formation of additional crosslinks induced by the CQDs, which also influence the crystallization behavior of the resin [22,31]. The nano-reinforcements which are relatively larger than CQDs act as nucleation sites, promoting the formation of larger crystalline domains within the polymer matrix. Conversely, in case of CQDs, which are much smaller derivatives, polymer interactions occur at a much smaller scale. Due to CQD reinforcement, excessive small-sized crystalline domains are formed, rather than larger ones. This modified network imparts enhanced toughness without compromising the composite's brittleness, making it ideal for applications demanding high energy absorption prior to failure [31]. However, excessive CQDs (0.7 %) can result in stacking and aggregation, which negatively impacts the composite's performance.

Flexural strength results revealed similar trend as observed for tensile strength, where composites incorporating 0.5 % CQDs exhibited significantly enhanced flexural strength [Fig. 4(c)]. A proportional increase in flexural strength was noted with increasing CQDs concentration up to this optimal level of 0.5 %. However, further increments in concentration led to a decline in flexural strength, as shown in Fig. 4(c). The neat sample displayed a maximum flexural strength of 29.7 MPa, while the inclusion of 0.5 % CQDs boosted this value to 45.2 MPa, representing a substantial 51 % enhancement. Notably, this significant improvement in flexural strength was achieved without compromising elongation, a critical mechanical property. The trend of increasing strength was observed up to the 0.5 % CQDs concentration threshold, beyond which a decrease in flexural strength was evident. Similarly, the flexural modulus of the composites followed a similar trend, increasing up to the 0.5 % concentration. The neat sample exhibited a flexural modulus of 996.15 MPa, which was linearly augmented by the addition of CQDs, as illustrated in Fig. 4(d). A peak flexural modulus of 1592.45 MPa was recorded for the sample containing 0.5 % CQDs. Furthermore, the modulus of the sample with 0.7 % CQDs was nearly identical to that of the neat sample. The optimal CQDs concentration resulted in a significant 60 % increase in flexural modulus compared to the neat sample.

The substantial enhancement in the flexural strength of the composites upon CQDs incorporation can be attributed to increased crosslinking and the formation of a more compact polymer network [29]. The synergistic effect of CQDs and nano-reinforcements leads to improved packing efficiency of polymer chains, consequently reducing voids and free volume within the polymer network. As reported previously [29], the increased density of the polymer network directly correlates with enhanced bending properties. The significant improvement in flexural modulus can be linked to the effective dispersion and strong interfacial interactions between CQDs and the polymer matrix. This optimized dispersion enables CQDs to act as efficient cross-linking agents, leading to a more rigid and robust polymer network. Notably, the CQDs synthesized in this study exhibit high compatibility with the unsaturated polyester resin, resulting in significant performance enhancements even at low concentrations. While higher loading concentrations of CQDs can further improve mechanical properties, excessive loading may lead to aggregation due to mutual stacking [48]. The optimal concentration of CQDs, where proper dispersion is achieved, provides the strongest barrier against void formation and maximizes the reinforcement effect [48].

Despite their lightweight and high specific strength, thermosetting fiber-reinforced composites suffer from inherently low impact strength due to their brittle and stiff nature. This limitation has hindered their application in areas demanding high toughness and impact energy absorption. While traditional carbon nano-reinforcements with relatively larger size, such as carbon nanotubes or graphene increase the stiffness, they often compromise toughness and impact resistance. In contrast, the results presented here demonstrate that CQDs, with their relatively smaller size and unique properties, can significantly enhance the impact strength of composites without compromising their elongation. This is in stark contrast to larger nano-reinforcements, which tend to increase brittleness and reduce impact energy absorption. As shown in Table 1 , the impact strength of the composites increased linearly with increasing CQDs concentration, reaching a maximum at 0.5 % CQDs. The 0.5 % CQDs sample exhibited a suitable 43 % increase in impact strength compared to the neat sample, from 14.32 KJ/m2 to 20.57 KJ/m2. This substantial enhancement can be attributed to the increased toughness of the CQDs-modified composite, which is achieved without compromising elongation.

The improved impact strength is primarily due to the matrix toughening mechanism facilitated by CQDs. These ultra-small carbon particles act as secondary nucleation sites, inducing crack branching upon impact [49]. In contrast, neat samples exhibit rapid propagation of primary cracks. The addition of CQDs can increase the stiffness of the composite material due to new interfaces formed between CQDs and matrix. However, excessive addition can lead to a decrease in toughness, which is due to aggregation of CQDs. Experimental results of mechanical performance show that 0.5 wt% CQDs provide the optimal balance between these two properties, maximizing reinforcement without compromising toughness. Based on maximally enhanced mechanical properties, i.e. tensile strength, flexural strength, and impact strength, 0.5 % concentration was considered optimal. Additionally, the CQDs-reinforced composite exhibits enhanced stress distribution without compromising elongation, delaying the onset of crack initiation. Hydrophobic nature of CQDs ensures strong interfacial interactions with the resin, leading to efficient load transfer and a 43 % increase in impact strength. However, excessive CQDs concentrations can hinder interfacial interactions and promote aggregation, leading to decreased impact strength. Therefore, careful optimization of the CQDs concentration is crucial to maximize impact resistance.

In summary, Table 1 shows that incorporating a minor concentration of CQDs significantly enhanced the mechanical properties of the composites. CQDs acted as effective energy dissipaters, improving mechanical strength while maintaining elongation. Previous studies have demonstrated that the enhanced resilience of CQD composites is attributed to mechanisms such as extensive shear band formation, nano-fibrillation, and crazing [22], which are most pronounced at optimal 0.5 % CQDs concentration in this work. This improved stress tolerance can be attributed to efficient stress transfer from the softer polymer matrix to the harder CQD reinforcement. In the absence of reinforcement, polymer chains are more susceptible to failure under stress. However, in the CQD-embedded polymer, stress is redirected towards the CQDs, enabling the composite to withstand higher loads [22]. Conversely, at higher CQD concentrations, agglomeration can occur, leading to stress concentration points that disrupt the uniform distribution of polymer chains and hinder the nucleation and crystallization processes [31].

Thermal properties of composites are important for their performance prediction under stress, shaping, thermal stresses, and performance aspects. Furthermore, the thermal properties of composites are important for testing their suitability in several high-tech structural applications. For example, automotive body parts, components are subjected to both thermal and dynamic mechanical stresses. To evaluate the suitability of CQD-modified composites for such demanding applications, DMA was performed. DMA provides valuable insights into the viscoelastic behavior of the polymer matrix and its variation by incorporating CQDs reinforcements. Specifically, the tan δ, which represents the ratio of loss modulus (viscous component) to storage modulus (elastic component), provides a quantitative measure of the viscoelastic behavior of the composite. Fig. 5 (a) illustrates the temperature-dependent tan δ values for the fabricated composite samples within a temperature range of 30–100 °C. A notable observation is the lower tan δ values observed in all CQD-containing composites compared to the neat resin, which is a characteristic behavior of nanocomposite systems [50]. The presence of CQDs within the polymer matrix effectively hinders the molecular mobility of polymer chains, thereby reducing energy dissipation through viscous deformation [50]. Furthermore, the incorporation of CQDs leads to a decrease in free volume within the polymer matrix, further contributing to the observed reduction in tan δ. This more compact arrangement of polymer chains, facilitated by the presence of CQDs, not only enhances the overall strength of the composite but also promotes a more elastic response during deformation, as evidenced by the lower tan δ values [50].

In addition, there is a systematic shift in the peak position with increasing CQD concentration. For composites containing lower CQD concentrations, such as 0.125 %, the tan δ peak is observed at relatively lower temperatures, approximately 50 °C. Conversely, composites with higher CQD loadings, like 0.75 %, exhibit a pronounced shift of the tan δ peak towards higher temperatures, reaching approximately 65 °C. This observed shift can be attributed to the enhanced crosslinking density and the formation of a more compact polymer network facilitated by the incorporation of CQDs [51]. Furthermore, this phenomenon underscores the excellent compatibility of the hydrophobic CQDs with the unsaturated polyester resin. The facile dispersion of CQDs within the matrix, likely driven by favorable van der Waals interactions, contributes significantly to the observed shift. This observation aligns with previous studies on hydrophobic graphite, which demonstrated a similar trend in tan δ peak shift [52]. In contrast, the poor compatibility of hydrophilic graphene oxide with the polyester resin resulted in negligible changes in the tan δ peak position [52]. The increased crosslinking density within the CQDs-reinforced composites contributes to this observed shift in the tan δ peak and plays a crucial role in enhancing the overall mechanical properties and thermal stability of the composite materials.

CQD-reinforced composites showed a distinct relaxation peak in dynamic mechanical analysis, with a prominent high-intensity peak centered around 60 °C. This peak corresponds to the primary relaxation (α-relaxation) of the cured unsaturated polyester resin network, commonly referred to as the glass transition temperature or softening point [53]. This transition is primarily governed by the motion of the polystyrene bridges within the polymer network, which form the essential backbone responsible for maintaining matrix integrity [53].

To investigate the differences in the polymer network caused by the inclusion of CQDs, the heat absorption and transfer characteristics were compared with those of the neat sample. Both samples were subjected to DSC, which revealed a significant difference in their heat absorption kinetics at a heating rate of 10 °C/min, as shown in Fig. 5(b). DSC is a simple yet powerful technique for analyzing key thermal properties, such as the glass transition temperature, specific heat capacity, and other kinetic events during heating, including curing and enthalpic relaxation of polymer chains. Mechanical characterization confirmed that optimal mechanical properties of the composites were achieved with a 0.5 % CQD inclusion, and thus, the sample with this concentration was selected for further testing. Heat flux DSC was employed to analyze the samples, where both the sample and reference are heated by the same source, and the difference in heat flow is measured. For endothermic or exothermic reactions, a positive or negative peak is observed, respectively.

In the temperature range of 110–150 °C, the DSC thermogram of the neat polymer sample displays a clear dip corresponding to the glass transition temperature (T g), as shown in Fig. S5. In contrast, this softening transition is significantly less pronounced in the CQD-reinforced composite, which can be attributed to reduced chain mobility within a more compact and constrained polymer network. This structural compaction is supported by the enhanced crystallinity observed in the XRD analysis of the composite (discussed in the following section). A similar suppression of T g has been reported in carbon nanotube-reinforced composites, where the intensity of the T g transition was diminished compared to the pristine matrix [54]. This phenomenon is commonly associated with the formation of a polymer–nanoparticle interphase region, which behaves as a rigid amorphous fraction [54]. In this region, polymer chains remain structurally amorphous yet are dynamically immobilized due to strong molecular interactions with both the crystalline domains and the dispersed nanofillers. In our case, the strong interfacial compatibility between the hydrophobic CQDs and the polymer likely promotes the formation of rigid amorphous domains. As a result, the overall heat capacity change at T g becomes too small to be detected by conventional DSC. Nevertheless, the presence of amorphous domains remains evident in the DMA analysis, where mechanical transitions associated with T g are still observable (discussed above).

In the temperature range of 200–250 °C, the curing process initiates, resulting in a second exothermic peak, as shown in Fig. S6. The peak intensity for the crosslinking process occurs at a lower temperature (209 °C) and shows lower intensity for the neat composite. In contrast, the crosslinking intensity is significantly higher for the CQD-reinforced composite, with a peak centered at a higher temperature of 235 °C. Additionally, the CQD composite exhibits a broader crosslinking peak, which is related to the increased melt viscosity of the polymer matrix due to the presence of CQDs [55]. As a result, higher energy input is required for the crosslinking process [55,56]. The temperature shift observed due to CQD inclusion highlights significant changes in the polymer structure, particularly due to higher crosslinking density and functionalization levels. The interactions between reactive functional groups and Van der Waals forces of the CQDs alter the viscous characteristics of the molten domains at higher temperatures [55,56]. Consequently, a high-intensity endothermic peak is observed, related to the devitrification of the CQD-modified resin during the thermal relaxation process [57]. In contrast, the neat sample shows an exothermic thermal degradation curve due to resin degradation, starting at temperatures above 280 °C. At temperatures above 380 °C, the thermal dynamics are influenced by the basalt fibers, which can tolerate high temperatures due to their inorganic ceramic nature.

The polymer chains within the composite matrix exhibit significant sensitivity to external energy applied, either as mechanical load or thermal energy. Upon exposure to temperature differences, these chains undergo rearrangement, leading to dimensional changes in the form of thermal expansion or contraction. These thermal dimensional changes can have a profound impact on the shape and mechanical integrity of composite components, particularly under thermal stress. Consequently, understanding the influence of CQDs on the thermal expansion behavior of the composite is crucial. The CTE was measured using a dilatometer over a temperature range of 30–150 °C, as depicted in Fig. 5(c). Given that 0.5 % CQD loading was identified as the optimal concentration, only the CTE of optimized CQDs composite structure was compared with neat composite.

The neat composite sample exhibited a relatively low initial expansion, followed by a sharp increase in CTE beyond the glass transition temperature. In contrast, the CQD-modified composite demonstrated a more linear and restrained expansion profile, particularly at higher temperatures [16]. This distinct thermal expansion behavior can be attributed to the higher chain mobility and free volume in the pristine polymer matrix of neat sample. However, incorporating CQDs restricts chain movement and reduces free volume, leading to a more constrained thermal expansion [58]. As a result, the CQD-modified composite exhibits a more stable and predictable thermal response, minimizing the risk of thermal stress-induced deformation and failure at higher temperatures.

SEM analysis revealed that the incorporation of CQDs significantly modified the interfacial microstructure of the composites [Fig. 6 (a,b)]. The CQDs-reinforced composite [Fig. 6(b)] exhibited improved fiber–matrix adhesion, as evidenced by a notable reduction in fiber pull-out compared to the neat sample [Fig. 6(b)]. High-resolution SEM images showed that the neat composite has larger and continuous microdomains due to a lower density of internal interfaces. In contrast, the composite containing 0.5 wt% CQDs displayed smaller microdomains, arising from the barrier effect of the CQD-rich interfaces [59]. This microstructural refinement increases the energy barrier for crack propagation by promoting frequent interfacial debonding and enhancing crack deflection. Consequently, stress dissipation occurs over a larger area, requiring greater energy input for crack propagation. The resulting fracture surface in CQDs-modified composites exhibited increased roughness compared to the relatively smooth surfaces of the neat composites, consistent with the mechanism of enhanced toughness via crack deflection and energy dissipation across numerous internal interfaces.

To gain deeper insight into the structural arrangement of polymer chains within single microdomain, XRD analysis was conducted in the 2θ range of 5–80°. As shown in Fig. 6(c), a pronounced increased intensity difference in diffraction peak appears around 2θ ≈ 20° in the 0.5 % CQD-modified unsaturated polyester resin (labeled: CQDs@UPR), indicating an increase in the crystallinity of polymer matrix. In contrast, our previous investigations involving hydrophilic CQDs with spherical morphology dispersed in polar polymer: polyvinyl alcohol (PVA), decreased the crystallinity [31]. The spherical shape of such CQDs induced a zigzag orientation PVA chains, resulting in reduced chain packing and consequently higher elongation at break. On the other hand, the 2D planer structure of the hydrophobic CQDs used in the current study does not promote such chain bending [33,60]. Instead, it facilitates improved molecular alignment and packing of polymer chains, thereby increasing the overall crystallinity. The enhanced compatibility between the non-polar polymer matrix and the hydrophobic CQDs further promotes strong interfacial interactions. These interactions encourage chain alignment along the planar surfaces of the CQDs, leading to the formation of more ordered domains. This phenomenon can be attributed to the nucleating effect of CQDs, which guide the compact arrangement of polymer chains around their surface. However, the micro-domains formed remain relatively small in CQDs modified polymer, as supported by SEM observations. Increased crystallinity within the polymer matrix correlates with improved inter-chain bonding and structural integrity, which ultimately contributes to superior mechanical performance of the composite material. Correlating the SEM and XRD data, the differences in polymer structure after incorporating CQDs is schematically compared in Fig. 6(d & e). Compared to neat sample, the CQDs modified resin has smaller domains, with compact crystal structure, which suppressed the crack generation and propagation.

The CQDs employed in this study exhibit surface passivation with nonpolar moieties such as CH, S-S, C-S, and aromatic functionalities from thiobenzoic acid. These structural features confer pronounced hydrophobicity, enabling favorable thermodynamic compatibility with the unsaturated polyester resin matrix, which itself contains hydrophobic –CH, –CH=CH–, and phenyl units within its backbone. At the molecular level, the nonpolar –CH groups on the CQDs can engage in London dispersion forces with the methylene and vinyl segments of the polymer chains, facilitating intimate molecular proximity and reducing interfacial free energy (Fig. 6(e)). Moreover, the aromatic π-systems present on both the CQDs and the polymer matrix can participate in π–π stacking interactions, further stabilizing the interface and promoting uniform dispersion throughout the resin (Fig. 6(e)). Dispersion stability of CQDs modified resin was checked for three days [Fig. S7], which showed dispersion stability for up to three days.

The synergistic combination of hydrophobic interactions, π–π stacking, and van der Waals forces contributes to the suppression of CQDs agglomeration and microphase separation—common challenges observed when incorporating hydrophilic nanofillers into nonpolar matrices. Compared to hydrophilic CQDs or oxygen-rich carbon-based nanomaterials like graphene oxide, the hydrophobic CQDs exhibit enhanced dispersibility without the need for surfactant mediation or surface functionalization. This intrinsic interfacial affinity is pivotal in promoting efficient stress transfer under mechanical loading and contributes to the observed enhancement in toughness and overall mechanical integrity of the nanocomposite. Table S1 highlights the advantages of proposed CQDs with hydrophilic CQDs and other carbon materials. Although the incorporation of hydrophobic CQDs enhances the crystallinity of the polymer matrix, no significant improvement in degradation dynamics was observed; for instance, TGA results shows almost similar rate of weight loss [Fig. S8]. This may be attributed to the inherently chemical nature of thermal degradation in resin, which primarily involves random scission of ester bonds. The extremely low filler loading and the lack of barrier or radical-scavenging behavior in CQDs further limit their influence on degradation behavior. Thus, while CQDs reinforce mechanical performance via crystallinity enhancement, their impact on stability remains minimal under the tested conditions. Specially for thermosetting resins like unsaturated polyester resin degrade through random chain scission and decomposition of ester groups, processes that are chemically driven and less influenced by physical chain ordering. Therefore, only those aspects of CQDs modified composites are superior which are strongly dependent on chain ordering.

Scalable and facile approach was used for CQDs synthesis, purified via filtration method, eliminating the need for time consuming purification via chromatography and dialysis. Characterization through TEM and XRD revealed the formation of circular CQDs with a size below 10 nm and a partially crystalline structure. XPS and FTIR analysis confirmed the hydrophobic nature of the CQDs and the presence of functional groups compatible with unsaturated polyester resin. This facilitated the facile dispersion of CQDs within the resin matrix using simple mechanical stirring and sonication. The incorporation of 0.5 wt% CQDs into the unsaturated polyester resin resulted in significant enhancements in mechanical properties. Notably, tensile strength increased by 72 %, flexural strength by 51 %, and impact strength by 43 % compared to the pristine composite. Furthermore, the tensile and flexural moduli of the optimized composite were 89 % and 60 % higher, respectively, than the neat sample. Importantly, these enhancements in mechanical strength were achieved without compromising elongation, suggesting that the CQDs effectively reinforce the polymer matrix while maintaining its ductility. Thermal analysis confirm that enhanced mechanical properties are related to change in polymer microstructure and chain packing. In the presence of CQDs, a compact polymer structure is formed, which restricts the chain mobility, resulting in enhanced thermal properties. Whereas the degradation dynamics of polymer composites are not significantly altered after incorporation of CQDs.

ce:biography.ce:simple-para: MumtazAli boasts a rich academic and research background. Currently, he is serving as a Research Professor at Hanyang University, funded by the Brain Pool Program Korea. Before this, he served as an Assistant Professor at National Textile University, Faislabad Pakistan with an experience of 3 years. His contributions in research earned him several national research grants, focused on the application of quantum dots in textiles and polymers. In recognition of his outstanding PhD thesis, he was honoured with the International Carbon Award in 2022 for “Advancement in Carbon Science and Technology”. This award also granted him an extended advisory position with Elsevier Carbon journal (IF 11.5). Based on his distinguished research, he received the “Best Researcher Award” from Hanyang University in 2020. Currently, focus of his current research lies in scalable and green synthesis of low dimensional carbon-rich catalysts, to replace metallic catalysts.

Muhammad Shoaib is a dedicated researcher and Ph.D. scholar in Advanced Materials at the National Textile University, Pakistan. Areas of his research interest include design and synthesis of advanced materials for flame retardant applications, sustainable textiles, and high-performance polymer composites. His research is adopted and commercialized by the local industries, which fulfils essence of his research motivation. He worked as a lead researcher in several national and industrial projects, resulting in patents and research publications in prestigious journals.

Hafsa Jamshaid is currently serving as Head of Textile Engineering Department at the National Textile University, Faisalabad (Pakistan), where she focuses on weaving and knitting technologies for high-performance textiles, technical fabrics, and composite reinforcements. An active scholar, she has published extensively in Web-of-Science-indexed journals and contributed several book chapters on advanced textile applications. Dr. Jamshaid is also a seasoned presenter, having delivered more than 100 papers at international conferences worldwide. Her teaching and research portfolio spans fabric-structure design, sustainable manufacturing, and multimodal quality characterization of textiles. In recognition of her contributions to the field, she was elected a Fellow of The Textile Institute, Manchester (United Kingdom). Dr. Jamshaid maintains wide-ranging collaborations with universities and industrial partners across Asia, Europe, and North America, leading projects that translate fundamental textile science into innovative technical products.

Min JaeKo is a professor at the Department of Chemical Engineering, Hanyang University, Korea, and director of BK21 Education & Research Center for Advanced Materials toward Carbon Neutrality. He is a member of The National Academy of Engineering of Korea. He received his BS and MS in Dept. of Fiber and Polymer Science and his Ph.D. in the School of Materials Sciences and Engineering from Seoul National University in 2001. He then performed his postdoc research at Dept. of Chemical Engineering, M.I.T. (2001-2004). Then, he joined Samsung Electronics Co. as a senior research engineer in 2005. He worked as a principal scientist at KIST (2008-2017) and joined Hanyang University in 2017. His research interests are focused on the development of materials and process for the next-generation energy-conversion devices. He was awarded.
